entries.title: Extending Burp: an overview of the approaches
entries.body:
# Extending Burp: an overview of the approaches

Out of the box, Burp covers most tasks, but sooner or later you run into "I want my own logic": my own filter, my own column, my own replacement rule, my own vulnerability check. Burp offers four levels of customization - from a couple of lines right in the interface to a full-fledged plugin in Java. The choice depends on how deep you need to go.

## Four paths

- **Bambdas** - mini-scripts in Java, written and run right in the Burp interface. No separate project, no build, no restart. Suitable for table filters, your own columns, match-and-replace rules, and custom actions in **Repeater**. The fastest way to "tweak" Burp to your needs.
- **Custom scan checks** - your own checks for **Burp Scanner** (Professional/DAST only). The scanner runs them together with the built-in ones. Written either in Java via the Montoya API or in the simplified **BCheck** language. Needed when you want to catch a vulnerability specific to your application without building a whole extension.
- **Extensions** - full-fledged plugins. Maximum control: intercepting and modifying traffic, integrating with third-party tools, access to Burp's internal tools, extending the UI. Installed from the **BApp Store** or loaded manually as a JAR.
- **Montoya API** - the modern Java API on which both extensions and part of Bambdas/scan checks are built. It has JavaDoc and example extensions on GitHub.

## How to choose

- Need a one-off filter/column/rule - a **Bambda**, a minute of work.
- Need a repeatable vulnerability check in the scanner - a **custom scan check** (BCheck for the simple, Java for the complex).
- Need to change Burp's behavior wholesale, hold state, draw your own tab - write an **extension** on the Montoya API.

> The principle: take the lightest tool that solves the task. Do not write a plugin where a five-line Bambda will do.

entries.title: Bambdas: mini-scripts right inside Burp
entries.body:
# Bambdas: mini-scripts right inside Burp

A Bambda is a short Java script that runs right inside Burp. No separate project, IDE, or JAR build: you write the code in the built-in editor, click **Apply** - and it works immediately. Because of this lightness, Bambdas are the workhorse for small customizations.

## Where they are used

One and the same mechanism covers several points in Burp:

- **View filters (table filters).** Your own logic for selecting rows in **Proxy > HTTP history**, **Proxy > WebSockets history**, **Target > Site map**, and in **Logger**. The script returns a `boolean`: `true` - show the row.
- **Custom columns.** Add computed columns to the **HTTP history**, **WebSockets history**, and **Logger** tables. The script returns a `String` - what you will see in the cell.
- **Match and replace rules.** Automatic modification of HTTP messages on the fly in the **Proxy**. The script returns the modified request or response.
- **Custom actions.** Extracting, transforming, and analyzing data in **Repeater** (up to and including calling AI).
- **Scan checks.** Your own vulnerability-detection logic for **Burp Scanner** (essentially a Bambda in the role of a custom scan check).

## Under the hood

Bambdas work through the **Montoya API**: the script has access to the current message object (for example `requestResponse`) and a set of utilities. Which objects exactly are available depends on the Bambda type and the place of use (a WebSockets filter, an HTTP-history column, and so on).

## Where to get them and where to store them

- **Bambda library** - a personal library of scripts inside Burp (the **Extensions > Bambda library** tab), which survives a restart and carries over between projects.
- Ready-made scripts - the official PortSwigger repository on GitHub (imported as `.bambda`).
- You can write from scratch right in Burp.

> A security note: a Bambda runs arbitrary code. Do not run scripts from unverified sources - this is effectively running someone else's code on your system.

entries.title: Managing the Bambda library
entries.body:
# Managing the Bambda library

All saved Bambdas live in one place - **Extensions > Bambda library**. This is the central store: from here scripts are reused across tools and projects, and here they are edited, copied, and deleted.

## What is visible in the library

Scripts are shown in a table with columns:

- **Name** - the script's name.
- **Function** - the task type: for example **View filter**, **Custom column**, **Match and replace**, **Custom action**, **Scan check**.
- **Location** - which tool it is intended for (**HTTP history**, **WebSockets history**, **Logger**, **Repeater**, and so on).
- **Last modified** - when it was last changed.

## Search and filtering

- Click **Search** and enter an expression - it will filter the list by text.
- The **Filter settings** panel lets you narrow the selection by **Function** and **Location**.

## Actions on a script

Select the script you need and use the buttons:

- **Edit** - open in the editor and adjust the code.
- **Copy** - make a copy (convenient as a starting point for a similar script).
- **Delete** - remove from the library.
- **Export** - export the selected scripts as `.bambda` files (to hand over to colleagues or for a backup).

## An important link to the scanner

If you edit a Bambda that defines a scan check, the changes will automatically propagate to the corresponding entry in the **Custom scan checks** library. That is, a scan-check Bambda lives "in two places" at once - edit it in one, and it updates in both.

> Keep meaningful names and use the filter by Function/Location - when dozens of scripts accumulate, without this the library turns into a junk heap.

entries.title: Importing Bambdas
entries.body:
# Importing Bambdas

You do not have to write everything yourself - most of the typical filters and actions have already been written by PortSwigger and the community. Ready-made Bambdas are distributed as `.bambda` files and imported into your library.

## Importing files and folders

1. Open **Extensions > Bambda library**.
2. Click **Import** - the **Import scripts** dialog will open.
3. Select one or more `.bambda` files or a whole folder of them.
4. Click **Open**.

Burp recursively traverses the selected folder and subfolders, finds all `.bambda` files, and adds them to the library.

## Importing from a GitHub repository

The official collection is the PortSwigger `bambdas` repository:

1. Download the repository as a ZIP.
2. Unpack the archive.
3. In **Extensions > Bambda library** click **Import**.
4. Point to the unpacked folder.
5. **Open** - Burp will scan the structure and pick up all the scripts it finds.

## Re-importing and updating

Each script is assigned a unique identifier when it is created/imported. If you import an updated version of a script you already have, Burp will offer to **replace** the existing version with the new one rather than breed duplicates.

> Security: a Bambda runs arbitrary code. Before importing from an unverified source, read what is inside the script - especially for custom actions and scan checks, which may send requests or call external services.

entries.title: Creating a Bambda
entries.body:
# Creating a Bambda

A Bambda can be created in two ways: from the library (universal, with a choice of function) or right in the tool you need (fast, the context is already set).

## From the library

1. **Extensions > Bambda library** -> **New** -> choose **Blank** (empty) or **From template** (with a starter template).
2. Enter a unique name.
3. Choose **Function** from the drop-down list - the task type (View filter, Custom column, Match and replace, Custom action, Scan check).
4. Choose **Location** - which Burp tool the script will work in.
5. Write the Java code.
6. **Save** - Burp will compile the script and save it to the library.

## Right in the tool

In places where a Bambda is applicable (a history filter, a custom column, match-and-replace, a custom action) there is a dedicated editor: you write the code, click **Apply** to apply it, and optionally **Save to library** to reuse it later.

## What the script returns - depends on the function

- **View filter** -> `boolean` (`true` - show the row).
- **Custom column** -> `String` (the text in the cell).
- **Match and replace** -> the modified request/response object.
- **Scan check** -> an audit result (`AuditResult`).

An example of an HTTP-history filter: show only redirects (3xx) with a session cookie:

```java
if (!requestResponse.hasResponse()) { return false; }
var response = requestResponse.response();
return response.isStatusCodeClass(StatusCodeClass.CLASS_3XX_REDIRECTION)
    && response.hasCookie("session");
```

An example of your own column: output the value of the **Server** header (or empty if there is no response/header):

```java
if (!requestResponse.hasResponse()) { return ""; }
var response = requestResponse.response();
return response.hasHeader("Server") ? response.headerValue("Server") : "";
```

## What to watch for

- Always check for the presence of a response (`hasResponse()`) before accessing `response()` - otherwise you will hit an error on requests without a response.
- Slow or resource-heavy scripts slow Burp down: the filter runs on every row, the column on every cell. Write concisely and without unnecessary allocations.

> The available objects (`requestResponse`, `Utilities`, and so on) depend on the chosen Function and Location. The full set of methods is in the Montoya API JavaDoc.

entries.title: Custom actions in Repeater
entries.body:
# Custom actions in Repeater

A custom action is a small, self-contained Bambda script in Java that runs on the current message in **Repeater** and does something useful with the data: extracts, transforms, analyzes. AI processing is also convenient to plug in here. The idea is quick "buttons" for routine operations you are too lazy to do by hand.

## How it works

- The script works with the request/response pair of the open Repeater tab.
- Ready-made actions are stored in your personal **Bambda library** and reused.
- In Repeater they are available through the **Custom actions** panel - you pick a saved script and run it on the current message.

## What the script consists of

The script has at its disposal the current message object (the request and response) and the Montoya API utilities. The typical skeleton: check that there is a response, pull out the piece you need (a header, the body, a token), do something with it, and return/output the result.

An example idea: get the value of the **Location** header from the response for subsequent processing:

```java
if (!requestResponse.hasResponse()) { return; }
var response = requestResponse.response();
if (response.hasHeader("Location")) {
    var location = response.headerValue("Location");
    // next: parse it, log it, pass it to another tool, etc.
}
```

## What to watch for

- A custom action is good for repeatable, uniform processing: pull out a CSRF token, decode a piece, normalize the body, run the response through AI analysis.
- The logic is the same as for other Bambdas: check `hasResponse()`, do not do heavy operations in a loop.
- For concrete building blocks and a working example, see the neighboring pages of the guide and the `bambdas` repository on GitHub - it has ready-made actions that are easier to adapt to your needs than to write from scratch.

> Custom actions are about speeding up routine in Repeater: write "get the token from the response" once, and from then on you click a button instead of copy-pasting by hand.

entries.title: Debugging Bambdas (troubleshooting)
entries.body:
# Debugging Bambdas (troubleshooting)

Errors in a Bambda come in two sorts: compilation errors (the code does not build) and runtime errors (it builds but crashes on real data). Burp helps catch both.

## Compilation errors

Burp checks the code as you write, even before applying it, and takes the context into account: the requirements depend on the Function and Location (for example, a view filter for WebSockets has its own permissible objects). You can see the problem in three ways:

1. Hover the cursor over the red underline in the editor - a description will pop up.
2. Click **Save** in the library editor - the errors will appear on the **Compilation errors** tab.
3. Click **Apply** in the editor inside a tool - the errors will also go to the **Compilation errors** tab.

## Runtime errors

These show up after the script is applied: a warning pops up in the corresponding Burp tool. To figure it out, open the script for editing and look at the error details, then fix the code.

## Logging for debugging

For diagnostics, use the `Logging` interface:

- `logging.logToOutput(...)` - ordinary debug messages.
- `logging.logToError(...)` - error messages (printed in red).

```java
if (!requestResponse.hasResponse()) {
    logging.logToError("No response - skipping");
    return false;
}
logging.logToOutput("Status: " + requestResponse.response().statusCode());
```

**An important limitation:** the `Logging` interface is not available from the **Bambda library** editor. The messages appear on the **Console** tab only when the script is applied via **Apply** from the editor inside a tool.

> In practice: write and debug the script in a specific tool's editor (where `logging` and the **Console** tab work), and save only the working version to the library.

entries.title: Custom scan checks: an overview
entries.body:
# Custom scan checks: an overview

Custom scan checks extend **Burp Scanner** with your own vulnerability-detection logic. The scanner runs them together with the built-in checks - this is a way to tailor scanning to a specific application and to react quickly to fresh vulnerabilities without shipping a full-fledged extension. This is a scanner capability - Professional/DAST.

## Two ways to get a check

- **Import a ready-made one** - take it from colleagues or from PortSwigger repositories (`bambdas` for Java checks and `BChecks` for BCheck checks).
- **Write your own** - in Java (via the Montoya API) for complex logic, or in the simplified **BCheck** language for typical cases.

## Java or BCheck - what to choose

- **BCheck** - a declarative mini-language: "send such a request, if there is a match in the response - raise an issue." Quick to write, reads almost like text, requires no API knowledge. Ideal for "send a payload - check the response" checks.
- **Java (Bambda)** - when you need non-trivial logic, working with several requests, complex conditions, fine-grained work with the body/timings.

## Active and passive

- **Passive** - only analyze already-received traffic, send no new requests. Cheap and safe (for example, "no CSP header").
- **Active** - send their own payloads into insertion points and look at the response (for example, injections). More expensive and more noticeable on the target.

## Storage and reuse

Checks are stored in the **Custom scan checks** library (the **Extensions > Custom scan checks** tab) and are reused across scans and projects. There is a built-in editor with templates and validation/debugging tools. Attaching checks to a scan is configured in the audit configuration.

> Security: a custom scan check runs arbitrary code and can send requests. Import checks only from trusted sources.

entries.title: Managing custom scan checks
entries.body:
# Managing custom scan checks

All your scanner checks are stored on the **Extensions > Custom scan checks** tab and are reused across projects. Here they are enabled, edited, copied, deleted, and exported.

## What is visible in the library

Checks are shown in a table. The main columns:

- **Name** - the check's name.
- **Author** - the author (filled in for a BCheck from the `author` field in the metadata).
- **Tags** - labels (for a BCheck they are automatically pulled from `tags`).

There is a search bar; the columns are configurable and sortable.

## Actions on a check

Select a check and use:

- **Edit** - open in the editor and adjust.
- **Copy** - make a copy (convenient as a starting point).
- **Delete** - remove from the library.
- **Export** - export to hand over: one check is saved as a file, several into a folder. The extensions: `.bcheck` for BCheck checks, `.bambda` for Java checks.

## Creating and importing

- New checks can be created right from the library (see the page on creating).
- Import - from `.bcheck`/`.bambda` files or from PortSwigger repositories on GitHub.

## The link to the Bambda library

Java checks (scan-check Bambdas) are visible both in the **Bambda library** and here. Editing in one place updates the entry in both - it is one and the same script.

> Security: checks run arbitrary code. Before using someone else's checks - especially active ones - review their contents.

entries.title: Importing custom scan checks
entries.body:
# Importing custom scan checks

Ready-made scanner checks are imported from files or whole folders. Supported:

- `.bcheck` - checks in the BCheck language;
- `.bambda` - Java checks containing a scan check function;
- folders with several check files.

## Importing files and folders

1. Open **Extensions > Custom scan checks**.
2. Click **Import**.
3. In the dialog select the file(s) or folder.
4. Click **Open**.

Burp finds all `.bcheck` and `.bambda` checks in the folder and subfolders and adds them to the library.

## Importing from GitHub

The official PortSwigger collections are the `BChecks` repository (BCheck checks) and `bambdas` (the `CustomScanChecks` section with Java checks):

1. Download the repository you need as a ZIP.
2. Unpack it.
3. **Extensions > Custom scan checks** -> **Import**.
4. Point to the unpacked folder -> **Open**.

## Re-importing and updating

Burp matches the imported checks against the ones you already have: by unique ID (for `.bambda`) or by file name (for `.bcheck`). On a match it will offer to **replace** the existing check with the new version.

> Security: custom scan checks run arbitrary code. Import and run checks only from trusted sources - active checks send real requests to the target.

entries.title: Creating a custom scan check
A guide to writing Java checks
entries.body:
# A guide to writing Java checks

A Java check (a scan-check Bambda) is built on the Montoya API. The type (active/passive) is determined not by a declaration but by how the code is written: whether you send new requests. Below are the key objects and methods.

## Returning a result - AuditResult

A check always returns an `AuditResult`:

```java
return AuditResult.auditResult();              // found nothing
return AuditResult.auditResult(issue1, issue2); // one or more issues
```

## Describing a vulnerability - AuditIssue

`AuditIssue.auditIssue(...)` packs the finding's metadata: title, detail, remediation, URL, severity, confidence, the problem background and the remediation background, and the request/response pair for display:

```java
AuditIssue.auditIssue(
    "TITLE", "DETAIL", "REMEDIATION",
    requestResponse.request().url(),
    AuditIssueSeverity.MEDIUM,
    AuditIssueConfidence.FIRM,
    "BACKGROUND", "REMEDIATION_BG",
    AuditIssueSeverity.MEDIUM,
    requestResponse
);
```

**Severity:** `INFORMATION`, `LOW`, `MEDIUM`, `HIGH`. **Confidence:** `TENTATIVE`, `FIRM`, `CERTAIN`.

## Working with the request/response pair - HttpRequestResponse

- `hasResponse()` - whether there is a response;
- `request()` / `response()` - access to the request/response;
- `timingData()` - timings (useful for time-based checks).

## Reading the response

```java
response.hasHeader("X-Frame-Options")
response.headerValue("X-Frame-Options")
response.body().indexOf(marker, false) // false = case-insensitive
```

## Modifying the request (for active checks)

```java
requestResponse.request()
    .withAddedParameters("name", "value")
    .withBody("content")
    .withAddedHeader("X-Test", "marker")
    .withMethod("POST");
```

## Insertion points and sending (active checks)

For an active check you take an insertion point, build a request with a payload, and send it:

```java
var checkRequest = insertionPoint.buildHttpRequestWithPayload(
    ByteArray.byteArray(payload, StandardCharsets.UTF_8)
);
var checkResponse = http.sendRequest(checkRequest); // returns HttpRequestResponse
```

## A mandatory practice - the emptiness check

So as not to breed errors and unnecessary work, before accessing the response check that it is present:

```java
if (!requestResponse.hasResponse()) {
    return AuditResult.auditResult();
}
```

> The check's logic is entirely up to you: a passive one reads what is already there, an active one sends `http.sendRequest(...)` and analyzes the response. Bind the issue to `request().url()` so that the finding lands on the correct URL.

entries.title: Testing custom scan checks
entries.body:
# Testing custom scan checks

Before letting a check loose in a real scan, you run it on controlled messages. Burp offers two ways: a quick one - in the built-in editor on specific requests, and a full one - a test scan with only your own checks.

## A test in the editor (quick)

Lets you run the check on specific HTTP messages without launching a full scan.

1. Open the check: **Extensions > Custom scan checks** -> **New** or **Edit**.
2. Select the target messages anywhere in Burp, right-click -> **Send to Custom scan checks editor**.
3. The **Select custom scan check test cases** panel will appear - here you manage the set of test cases.
4. Use the checkboxes to enable/disable individual cases and click **Run test**.

**The results** show the number of requests and issues, plus four tabs: **Audit items**, **Issues**, **Event log**, and **Logger**. A case's contents can be edited right on the **Request** and **Response** tabs and the test re-run.

## A test scan (only your own checks)

When you have several checks and want to test them "in battle," run a scan with the built-in checks disabled:

1. In the scan launcher open the **Scan configuration** tab.
2. **Audit configuration > Scan checks**.
3. On the **Built-in** tab disable the built-in checks.
4. On the **Custom** tab enable only your own.

This way the scan results will show exactly the work of your checks, without noise from the standard ones.

> Discipline: first the editor on 2-3 control requests (including a deliberately vulnerable one and a deliberately clean one), then a test scan. This catches both false positives and misses before the check makes it into a real project.

entries.title: BCheck: check definitions
entries.body:
# BCheck: check definitions

BCheck is your own check for **Burp Scanner**, described in a compact declarative language. The scanner runs BChecks together with the built-in checks. A definition is an ordinary text file with the `.bcheck` extension, which reads almost like a human script: "under such a condition send a request, if there is a match in the response - raise an issue."

## Structure

1. A **metadata block** (`metadata`) - mandatory and comes first.
2. (optional) **`define`** - variables of inner scope.
3. (optional) **`run for each`** - repeat the check for each value of an array.
4. Exactly one **`given ... then`** block - the main logic.

## Metadata

```
metadata:
    language: v2-beta
    name: "Simple SQLi"
    description: "Tests insertion points for basic SQLi"
    author: "Peter Wiener"
    tags: "sql", "active", "sql-injection"
```

The `name`, `author`, `tags` fields are pulled into the **Custom scan checks** library (the **Name**, **Author**, **Tags** columns).

## The given/then block

`given` specifies what the check hooks onto: `given response`, `given request`, `given host`, `given path`, `given <type> insertion point` (for example `given any insertion point`, the `query`/`header`/`body`/`cookie` variants, which can be joined with `or`).

The main directives inside: `send request` (send a request, optionally `called <name>` so you can later reference `<name>.response`), `send payload` (insert a payload into the insertion point), `if ... then ... end if` (+ `else if`, `else`), and `report issue` / `report issue and continue`. The first ends the check, the second raises an issue and continues (convenient when one BCheck catches several findings; the name is set by the `name` field).

**Conditions:** `{X} matches "regex"`, `"string" in {X}`, `{X} is {Y}`, `{X} differs from {Y}`; Collaborator detection - `dns interactions` / `http interactions` / `smtp interactions`; the connectives `and`, `or`, `not(...)`. For `report issue` you specify `severity` (`info`/`low`/`medium`/`high`), `confidence` (`certain`/`firm`/`tentative`), as well as `detail`/`remediation`.

## Example: a passive check

Raise an info issue if the Apache Struts version flashes by in the response:

```
metadata:
    language: v2-beta
    name: "Apache Struts version disclosure"

given response then
    if {latest.response} matches "Apache Struts [\d\s\.]+" then
        report issue:
            severity: info
            confidence: certain
            detail: "Disclosed Apache Struts version"
            remediation: "Do not reveal version information in responses"
    end if
```

## Example: a host-level one with path enumeration

We look for an exposed `.git` directory across several paths (`run for each`):

```
metadata:
    language: v1-beta
    name: "Exposed git directory"
    description: "Tests for exposed git directory"
    author: "Carlos Montoya"

run for each:
    potential_path = "/.git/config", "/.git/config~"

given host then
    send request called check:
        method: "GET"
        path: {potential_path}

    if "[core]" in {check.response.body} then
        report issue:
            severity: info
            confidence: certain
            detail: `Git directory found at {potential_path}.`
            remediation: "Ensure your git directories are not exposed."
    end if
```

## What to watch for

- Backticks (`` ` ``) - interpolation: variables like `{potential_path}` are substituted inside. Double quotes - an ordinary string.
- In the metadata you encounter the language versions `v1-beta` and `v2-beta`; for new checks, target the current one (`v2-beta`).
- Ready-made BChecks - the PortSwigger `BChecks` repository on GitHub; adapt a ready-made one to your needs, it is faster than writing from scratch.
- Before using, run the check through **Validate** and a test on control messages.

> BCheck covers 80% of the "send - check the response" needs without a single line of Java. Take complex multi-step logic into a Java check.
